Target False-Ad Suit Challenging Sheet Thread Count Gets Revived. A federal appeals court has revived a class-action lawsuit against Target, challenging its labeling of "100% cotton" 800-thread count bed sheets. The Ninth Circuit reversed a lower court's dismissal, ruling that the ambiguity of the label needed further analysis to determine if a reasonable consumer would be deceived. The plaintiff, Alexander Panelli, claimed the sheets were rougher than advertised and independent tests showed discrepancies in the thread count.

Target False-Ad Suit Challenging Sheet Thread Count Gets Revived. A federal appeals court has revived a class-action lawsuit against Target, which alleges the retailer falsely advertised "100% cotton" 800-thread count bed sheets. The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that the lower court failed to determine if the label was ambiguous, thereby requiring further proceedings. The suit was brought by a plaintiff who found the sheets to be rougher than expected, with independent testing reportedly contradicting the advertised thread count.

Target False-Ad Suit Challenging Sheet Thread Count Gets Revived. A federal appeals court has revived a class-action lawsuit against Target (NYSE: TGT) regarding claims of false advertising for its "100% cotton" 800-thread count bed sheets. The case will now return to the lower court for further proceedings.

Starbucks Wins 5th Circ. Bid To Scrap NLRB Subpoena Order. The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals has vacated a National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) order against Starbucks. The court ruled that the NLRB applied an incorrect legal standard when determining if Starbucks committed an unfair labor practice by issuing what the board deemed overbroad subpoenas to pro-union employees. This decision marks a win for Starbucks in its challenge against the NLRB regarding subpoena practices.

Starbucks Wins 5th Circ. Bid To Scrap NLRB Subpoena Order. The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals has overturned a National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) order against Starbucks. The NLRB had previously cited Starbucks for issuing overly broad subpoenas to pro-union employees. The court's decision, issued on Friday according to the article, stated that the NLRB applied an incorrect standard.

Starbucks Wins Fifth Circuit Challenge to NLRB Subpoena Ruling. The US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit has ruled that the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) was wrong to find Starbucks Corp. in violation of federal labor law due to overly broad subpoenas issued to workers. The court stated that the NLRB incorrectly applied a legal test for quashing subpoenas to determine civil liability. The case has been sent back to the NLRB for reconsideration under the correct legal standard, which focuses on whether the company's conduct was coercive based on the totality of circumstances.

Disney debuts giant screen brand for cinemas to rival Imax. Walt Disney Co. is launching a new large-format screen certification called Infinity Vision to compete with Imax, aiming to capture a larger share of the immersive moviegoing experience market. This initiative is timed with the release of "Avengers: Doomsday" in December, a film that doesn't have exclusive access to Imax screens due to a scheduling conflict. The Infinity Vision standard requires specific screen dimensions, laser projection, and Dolby 7.1 surround sound, a criteria which nearly 5,500 screens globally could meet.

Disney’s Ongoing Guest Service Recovery After 60% of Cast Members Hired for Reopening. Disneyland is actively working to recover its guest service standards after a significant portion of its Cast Members were new hires post-reopening, many lacking prior park experience. The company is implementing enhanced training, reinforcing service expectations, and empowering Cast Members with more autonomy to improve guest experiences, leading to better morale and lower turnover. These efforts are aimed at restoring the "magic" that guests felt had slipped since 2019, recognizing that Cast Members are crucial to the overall Disney experience.
